metadata.title: Deploy a Keyword Spotter: Find the Layer That Leaked Accuracy

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The idea that toolchain conversion, not quantization, leaked those three points of accuracy changed who I blame first. Using a per-layer MSE diff between the float model and the TFLite interpreter: (1) walk me through the bisection that localizes the divergence spike to layer seven, (2) show me how to tell normal quantization noise from a true toolchain failure like an unsupported fusion or a CMSIS-NN fallback, (3) add a per-op supported-check so I can see which specific operator at layer seven rounds differently than the float version.

props.command: The idea that toolchain conversion, not quantization, leaked those three points of accuracy changed who I blame first. Using a per-layer MSE diff between the float model and the TFLite interpreter: (1) walk me through the bisection that localizes the divergence spike to layer seven, (2) show me how to tell normal quantization noise from a true toolchain failure like an unsupported fusion or a CMSIS-NN fallback, (3) add a per-op supported-check so I can see which specific operator at layer seven rounds differently than the float version.
props.segment: Deploy a Keyword Spotter: Find the

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes -- BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: The model was ninety-four percent accurate in Python. After the toolchain got done with it, it was ninety-one percent -- and nobody could say where the three percent went. Quantization is the obvious suspect, but toolchain-induced accuracy loss is a separate failure: an unsupported op fusion, a range mismatch, a CMSIS-NN fallback that silently changes the output. The only defense is a diff at every hop. Bisect which layer introduced the divergence before you blame the wrong thing.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 94% in Python, 91% after toolchain; toolchain loss vs quantization loss as separate failures; bisection as the diagnostic

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write deploy_runner.py, convert a Keras model to TFLite int8, run the TFLite interpreter on a validation set, compare it to the float model, and bisect which layer or op introduced the largest output divergence.

props.command:
claude "write deploy_runner.py:
  convert keras model to TFLite int8,
  run TFLite interpreter on val set,
  compare to float model,
  bisect which layer/op introduced
  the largest output divergence."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The bisection walks the network layer by layer and compares the float model's intermediate output against the TFLite interpreter's output at the same point. Divergence is mean squared error between the two. A small divergence is normal quantization noise. A spike is a toolchain failure -- an unsupported fusion or a kernel that rounds differently than the float version.

props.code:
# deploy_runner.py  --  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def bisect_divergence(float_outputs, tflite_outputs):
    # float_outputs, tflite_outputs: list of arrays,
    # one per layer, on the same val batch
    divergences = []
    for i, (f_out, t_out) in enumerate(
            zip(float_outputs, tflite_outputs)):
        mse = np.mean((f_out - t_out) ** 2)
        divergences.append((i, mse))
    spike = max(divergences, key=lambda x: x[1])
    return divergences, spike

# spike = (layer_idx, mse_value)
# -> locate the leaking op at that layer

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Run it. Ten layers plotted left to right, divergence on the vertical axis. Layers zero through six: small bars, normal quantization noise. Layer seven: a crimson spike. MSE jumps by two orders of magnitude. The bisection localizes the leak. This is the layer the toolchain broke, not quantization in general.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: per-layer divergence bars, layers 0-6 in SLATE (low), layer 7 in CRIMSON (spike). Chip: LAYER 7: DIVERGENCE SPIKE.

beats.narration_text: Layer seven is the spike. Now find out why. Iterate: claude -- add a per-op supported and unsupported check for CMSIS-NN and flag any fallbacks.

props.command:
claude "add a per-op supported/unsupported
  check for CMSIS-NN and flag fallbacks"

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Re-run with op support check. Above each layer bar, a chip appears: OK for layers with CMSIS-NN kernel support. At layer seven: FALLBACK. The op at that layer -- a fused activation that CMSIS-NN doesn't support -- falls back to a reference kernel that rounds differently than the float model. That is where the three percent went.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same divergence bars, OK chips above layers 0-6 and 8-9 (INK), FALLBACK chip at layer 7 (CRIMSON). Final chip: CMSIS-NN FALLBACK ACCURACY LEAK.

beats.narration_text: The six-step deploy pipeline breaks silently between steps. Quantization error is one thing. Toolchain error is another. The bisection separates them by walking the network and measuring the divergence at each hop. The CMSIS-NN op check explains the mechanism. Fix the op, rebuild, and the three percent comes back.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: quantization error vs toolchain error; bisection as the separation tool

beats.narration_text: Your move: bring a trained keyword-spotter model in Keras or TFLite format and a validation set -- Claude can't train these, but given them it does the conversion, bisection, and op check in pure Python, no hardware required. The output beats in this video use illustrative divergence values. Run deploy_runner.py on your real model to get the honest layer-by-layer diff.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) bring a trained model + val set, 2) run deploy_runner.py, 3) check bisection output for the leaking op

Toolchain error is another. The bisection separates them by walking the network and measuring the divergence at each hop. The CMSIS-NN op check explains the mechanism. Fix the op, rebuild, and the three percent comes back. beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
